data.title: Taiwan to be one of first to talk tariffs with US, president says
data.description:
Key points:
  • Taiwan hopes to speed ahead with U.S. tariffs talks
  • Taiwan had due to be hit with 32% tariff
  • Neither Taiwan nor U.S. have given timeframe for talks

Taiwan is to be included in the first batch of trading partners to hold talks with Washington, President Lai Ching-te said on Friday, as his economy minister said the island could import over $200 billion worth of U.S. goods, much of it in energy.

U.S. President Donald Trump said on Wednesday he would temporarily lower the hefty duties he had just imposed on dozens of countries while further ramping up pressure on China. Taiwan, a major semiconductor producer, was due to be hit with 32% tariffs.

Countries around Asia have already said they have begun or are preparing to begin talks with Washington over the tariffs, and the White House has said nearly 70 countries have reached out to begin negotiations.

"We are in the first batch of negotiations, and the government will be well prepared," Lai told business leaders in the central Taiwanese city of Taichung, in comments broadcast live by local media, without offering a timeframe.

"Taiwan's desire to strengthen economic and trade cooperation with the United States over the past years can be (fulfilled) by taking this opportunity," he added.

The office of the United States Trade Representative did not immediately respond to a request for comment sent outside of office hours in Washington.

Speaking to reporters at parliament in Taipei, Economy Minister Kuo Jyh-huei, who said on Thursday Taiwan could buy $200 billion more from the U.S. over 10 years and increase LNG imports as part of a trade deal, said it could be more than that amount.

"This is just the part from the economy ministry," he said, adding much of the imports could be energy-related.

Taiwan has not said when formal talks with the United States might start.

But Foreign Minister Lin Chia-lung told reporters he hoped a consensus with the United States could be reached within 90 days.

"Taiwan and the United States have been communicating smoothly and have continued to make arrangements for the tariff talks," he said.

President Lai on Sunday offered zero tariffs as a basis point for talks with the United States, saying Taiwan would buy more from and invest more in the country.

Taiwan, home to the world's largest contract chipmaker TSMC , has long sought a free trade deal with the United States.

The United States is Taiwan's most important international backer and arms supplier, despite the absence of formal diplomatic ties.

Taiwan faces increase military and political pressure from its giant neighbour China, which views the democratically governed island as its own territory. Taiwan's government rejects those claims, saying only the island's people can decide their future.

data.title: Tesla stops taking new orders in China for two imported, US-made models
data.description:
Tesla has suspended taking new orders for Model S and Model X vehicles on its Chinese website, Reuters checks showed on Friday, as the world's two largest economies exchange blows in a trade war.

New orders for the two imported models were also no longer available on the U.S. automaker's WeChat mini programme account Both models are made in the United States and imported to China.

The company did not give a reason for the move, which comes after China imposed tariffs of 84% on U.S. goods in response to Trump's levies on Chinese imports that now total 145%.

Tesla did not immediately respond to a request for comment.

The U.S. automaker makes Model 3 and Model Y cars at its plant in the commercial hub of Shanghai, to be sold in China and exported to markets such as Europe, making up the vast majority of its sales.

China imported 1,553 Model X cars and 311 Model S cars in 2024, said Li Yanwei, an analyst with the China Auto Dealers Association.

data.title: South Korea's Kospi Falls 0.5%, Dragged by Auto, Battery StocksMarket Talk
data.description: South Korea's benchmark Kospi fell 0.5% to close at 2432.72, dragged by auto and battery stocks. The index lost 1.3% for the week. Foreign investors were net sellers in Friday's session after declines on Wall Street overnight. The U.S.-China trade tensions remain elevated following a clarification by the White House that U.S. tariffs on China total 145% and not 125%. Carmakers Hyundai Motor and Kia slid 5.1% and 7.0%, respectively, as 25% U.S. levies on car imports continued to weigh on sentiment. USD/KRW settled 0.4% lower at 1,449.90 in Seoul onshore trading. South Korea's 10-year government bond yield was up 2.6 bps at 2.728%. (kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com)

data.title: Market Chatter: EU Leaders Set to Visit Beijing for Summit with Xi Jinping
data.description:
Top European Union officials are expected to visit Beijing in late July for a summit with Chinese President Xi Jinping, The South China Morning Post reported Friday, citing sources.

Despite the usual rotation of summit locations, Xi's reluctance to travel to Brussels has led European Council President Antonio Costa and European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen to agree to the trip, according to the report.

While no official date has been confirmed by China, the planned visit signals the EU's intent to strengthen ties with Beijing amid strained relations with the US, the SCMP wrote.

Disclaimer

Market Chatter news is derived from conversations with market professionals globally. This information is believed to be from reliable sources but may include rumor and speculation. Accuracy is not guaranteed.

data.title: South Korean shares fall for third straight week as tariff woes persist
data.description:
Key points:
  • KOSPI falls, foreigners net sellers
  • Korean won strengthens against dollar
  • South Korea benchmark bond yield falls
  • For the midday report, please clicknL2N3QP03F

Round-up of South Korean financial markets:

** South Korean shares fell on Friday, tracking Wall Street's overnight losses amid worries about U.S. tariffs, and posted a third straight weekly loss.

** The benchmark KOSPI closed down 0.50% at 2,432.72.

** The index fell 1.3% this week, after a 3.6% decline in the prior week.

** U.S. President Donald Trump's trade war rattled global markets anew on Thursday as stocks and oil prices sank due to fears that China may once again respond with higher tariffs match the latest U.S. levies.

** South Korean Finance Minister Choi Sang-mok said on Friday the government will strengthen monitoring of foreign exchange, bonds and money markets while preparing contingency plans under varying scenarios as uncertainty over a global trade war persists.

** "Markets will continue to react to every word Trump says next week," said Seo Sang-young, an analyst at Mirae Asset Securities.

** Among index heavyweights, chipmaker Samsung Electronics fell 2.13% and peer SK Hynix lost 1.31%, while battery maker LG Energy Solution slid 4.01%.

** Hyundai Motor shed 5.08%, and sister automaker Kia Corp lost 7.03%.

** Of the total 936 traded issues, 537 shares advanced, while 343 declined.

** Foreigners were net sellers of shares worth 688.9 billion won ($475 million).

** The won was quoted at 1,449.9 per dollar on the onshore settlement platform , 0.39% higher than its previous close.

** In money and debt markets, June futures on three-year treasury bonds (KTBc1) rose 0.09 point to 107.42.

** The most liquid three-year Korean treasury bond yield (KR3YT=RR) fell 1.5 basis points to 2.419%, while the benchmark 10-year yield fell 0.7 bps to 2.704%.

($1 = 1,450.1900 won)

data.title: China Stocks Rise on Signs of State-Backed Market Support
data.description:
The Shanghai Composite gained 0.45% to close at 3,238, while the Shenzhen Component advanced 0.82% to 9,834 on Friday, extending their winning streak to a fourth straight session.

Optimism around potential state-backed equity purchases helped offset concerns about an intensifying Sino-U.S. trade war.

Beijing ramped up efforts to stabilize domestic markets this week, urging state-owned financial institutions to increase equity investments.

A flurry of share buyback announcements from listed companies also bolstered investor confidence.

Meanwhile, the Trump administration confirmed that cumulative U.S. tariffs on Chinese goods have climbed to 145%, prompting fears of further retaliation from Beijing, which has already imposed an 84% levy on U.S. imports.

Reports also suggest that China is preparing new countermeasures targeting American firms while strengthening trade relations with the EU. Growth-focused sectors such as technology and new energy led the day’s gains.

data.title: Landmark Immigration Opens New Branch Officer in New Delhi, India; Shares Up 3%
data.description:
Landmark Immigration Consultants has opened a new branch office at in New Delhi, India, according to a BSE filing on Friday.

Shares of the company were up nearly 3% in recent trade.

data.title: Scania Buys Northvolt Systems Industrial Unit
Department store of Croatia's bankrupt Nama to be put up for sale
data.description:
ZAGREB (Croatia), April 11 (SeeNews) - A department store of bankrupt Croatian retailer Nama in Zagreb with an estimated value of 34.5 million euro ($38.5 million) will be put up for sale via the electronic auction system of the state financial services agency Fina, the Zagreb commercial court said.

The department store, located at the main square in the centre of Zagreb, cannot be sold at a price lower than 75% of its estimated value in the first round of the auction, 50% in the second auction, 25% in the third auction, the commercial court said in its decision dated April 8. If the asset is not sold in the first three auctions, in the fourth auction the starting price would be set at one euro.

Nama has continued to operate during the bankruptcy procedure and has some 160 employees. The future buyer is required to continue its business and keep all of the existing employees for two years from the day of taking over the property.

($ = 0.896 euro)

data.title: Turkey Retail Sales Ease to 7-Month Low
data.description:
Retail sales in Turkey rose by 12.2% year-on-year in February 2025, slowing from an upwardly revised 12.6% gain in the previous month.

This marked the weakest growth in retail trade since July 2024, as sales increased at a softer pace for food, beverages and tobacco in specialized stores (19.6% vs 20.2% in January), information and communication equipment in specialized stores (8.5% vs 16.6%), cultural and recreation goods in specialized stores (3% vs 10%), other household equipment in specialized stores (3.5% vs 8.9%), and sales via stalls and markets (10% vs 14%).

In addtion, sales decreased for automotive fuel in specialized stores (-0.5% vs 4%).

Meanwhile, sales accelerated for other goods in specialized stores (24.5% vs 21.9%) and sales not in stores, stalls/markets (6% vs 3.4%).

On a seasonally adjusted monthly basis, retail trade eased to 1.1% in February 2025 from 2% in the previous month.

data.title: Central China Management's Sales Fell 29% in Q1
data.description:
Central China Management recorded sales of projects under management of 2.69 billion yuan in the first quarter, down 29% from 3.80 billion yuan in the year-ago period, according to a Thursday filing with the Hong Kong exchange.

Contracted sales area declined 28% to 460,210 square meters from 636,992 square meters.

The average monthly selling price declined 1.9% to 5,844 yuan per square meter from 5,957 yuan per square meter a year earlier.

The company secured one newly contracted project in March with a gross floor area of 27,400 square meters.

data.title: Siyaram Recycling Bags Order Worth $1.2 Million; Shares Up 4%
data.description:
Siyaram Recycling Industries has secured an order worth $1.2 million from Hong Kong-based Greenland Trading, according to a Friday filing to the BSE.

The contract is for the supply of 200 tons of brass billets that will be exported to China within 45 days.

Shares of the company were up 4% in recent trade.

data.title: Nikkei Falls 3.0%, Dragged by Electronics, Pharma, Auto StocksMarket Talk
data.description: Japanese stocks ended lower as growing U.S.-China trade tensions weighed on the global economic outlook. Electronics, pharmaceutical and auto shares led the losses. Sony Group dropped 7.4%, Chugai Pharmaceutical lost 6.4%, and Nissan Motor shed 6.3%. The Nikkei Stock Average fell 3.0% to 33585.58. Tariff-related headlines remain in focus. USD/JPY is at 144.13, down from 144.44 as of Thursday 5 p.m. Eastern time. The 2-year Japanese government bond yield dropped 7 bps to 0.605%, the 10-year yield fell 2.5 bps to 1.345% while the 20-year yield rose 6 bps at 2.365%. (kosaku.narioka@wsj.com; @kosakunarioka)

data.title: U.K. Economy Shows Signs of Strength But Tariffs Darken Outlook2nd Update
data.description:
By Joshua Kirby

The U.K. economy is growing more strongly than expected but U.S. President Trump's barrage of tariff hikes and pauses threaten to stymie further acceleration ahead.

Gross domestic product increased by 0.5% in February, figures from the Office for National Statistics showed Friday. That was a better performance than the marginal growth economists had expected, and means the U.K. economy is very likely to record growth over the first quarter as a whole, barring an unexpectedly large reversal in March.

February's growth was driven by strong output from Britain's factories, with manufacturing production unexpectedly rising 2.2% on month on the back of a rise in output from auto plants, as well as in electronics and pharmaceuticals, the ONS said.

Still, strength in manufacturing may be in part down to firms seeing a temporary rush of orders aiming to beat Washington's planned tariffs.

"It's possible the industrial sector is benefiting from tariff front-running," Capital Economics' Ruth Gregory said in a note.

"February's resilience is unlikely to last," she said, pointing to the wider global economic malaise stemming from uncertainty around Washington's trade policy.

After Trump last week unveiled a sweeping package of tariffs on goods imported into the U.S., London's FTSE 100 index suffered four straight days of losses, enduring on Monday its worst trading since the Covid-19 pandemic. Stocks rallied later in the week after Trump agreed to halt a central part of the tariff package amid the turmoil roiling markets at home and across the globe.

But a 10% tariff will remain in place on U.K. goods, as will a separate 25% duty on cars and metals. At more than 15% of the total, the U.S. is the largest export market for the U.K. goods, of which more than 58 billion pounds ($75.23 billion) worth crossed the Atlantic to America in the 12 months to last September, according to figures from the government's business department.

That makes the U.K. less exposed to tariffs than some neighbors, such as Germany, which sent 160 billion euros of goods to the U.S. last year, or Ireland, home to the operations of many American drugmakers. British goods exports also pale next to services exports to the U.S., worth some 124 billion pounds a year, and not liable to Trump's levies. But even a 10% tariff could knock Britain's already fragile economic growth.

And policymakers at the Bank of England could be forced into cutting interest rates more quickly than they had foreseen in a bid to lift some of the smog clouding activity in the country.

"The damaging aftereffects from this [tariff] turbulence will be felt for some time, with the resulting global uncertainty likely to further undermine the U.K. economy," said Suren Thiru, economics director at the Institute of Chartered Accountants in England and Wales. Businesses will also suffer from an increase in payroll costs from April, Thiru noted.

"The greater global financial and economic instability caused by the US tariff announcements makes a May rate cut look more likely than not, by further fuelling rate setters' concerns over the underlying resilience of the U.K. economy," Thiru said.

A more general trade war is likely to mean slower growth ahead for the U.K. economy Sustained high tariffs on British goods entering the U.S. market, as well as indirect effects from a chillier global trade landscape, could mean GDP expanding just 0.7% this year, compared with 1.5% in case tariffs were not implemented, according to calculations by the National Institute of Economic and Social Research, a think tank. Growth would be even feebler next year in that scenario, the NIESR said in a report.

"History shows how devastating a full-scale tariff war can be," NIESR economist Ahmet Kaya said.

The government last week said it would invite British businesses to shape the response to tariffs, and began compiling a list of American goods that could be targeted in retaliation.

"We are pressing the case for a trading relationship that supports businesses on both sides of the Atlantic," trade minister Jonathan Reynolds said.

Prime Minister Keir Starmer, meanwhile, this week said New York-based entertainment giant Comcast NBCUniversal would build a new theme park outside London, an investment he said would boost the British economy by billions of pounds.

"It will boost our world-class creative industries," he said. "It will drive growth across the country."
